oneLiner: This work matters because CUNY was founded in 1847 as the country's first free public higher-education institution, explicitly so the children of the poor and the rich could sit in the same classroom, and it remains one of the most powerful engines of economic mobility for low-income New Yorkers today.
summary: CUNY traces back to the Free Academy, chartered in 1847 with founder Townsend Harris's mandate to "open the doors to all" regardless of wealth, at a time when higher education was reserved for the affluent. Tuition stayed free for 129 years until New York City's 1975-76 fiscal crisis forced the policy's end. Today, nearly half of CUNY students come from households earning less than $20,000 a year, and research has found CUNY propels roughly six times as many low-income students into the middle class and beyond as all Ivy League colleges combined. Programs like CUNY ASAP have shown dramatically higher graduation rates than the university average, which is part of why the University has continued to invest in closing equity and completion gaps.

sources.label: Origins and Formative Years - The City University of New York
New Study Confirms CUNY's Power As National Engine Of Economic Mobility - CUNY
